one_line_takeaway: A 2024 taxonomy survey mapping how knowledge distillation is used inside federated learning across five challenge areas (heterogeneity, client-drift, catastrophic forgetting, communication, privacy); useful as an AI-security reference for its Sec. IV.E / Table VI review of FL privacy attacks and KD-based defenses, but it is a literature synthesis, not security research or an empirical study.
key_finding: The synthesizing claims: (1) swapping parameter exchange for logit/soft-label exchange simultaneously addresses several FL pain points at once (communication cost, model heterogeneity, and a reduced privacy attack surface), which is the unifying reason KD is attractive inside FL; (2) FD's benefits are consistently bounded by the public/auxiliary or synthetic data — when the proxy distribution diverges from the true global distribution, global-model accuracy degrades, and data-free generators themselves inherit the Non-IID heterogeneity they aim to fix; (3) across every challenge the survey flags an accuracy-vs-efficiency/privacy trade-off (compressing or randomly selecting transmitted logits can drop crucial training signal); (4) surveyed FD work is overwhelmingly restricted to classification tasks that emit logits, limiting FD's applicability to tasks like detection, segmentation, and tracking.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical or methods paper). Its own stated contribution is the taxonomy/synthesis: the authors position it as 'the first systematic review of KD strategies applied to FL,' organizing the Federated Distillation (FD) literature around five FL challenges: (1) heterogeneity (data / system / model), (2) client-drift, (3) catastrophic forgetting, (4) communication costs, and (5) privacy, followed by an applications map (industrial engineering/IoT, computer vision, NLP, healthcare). The contribution is the organizing structure and the challenge-to-technique mapping, NOT any of the surveyed methods. FD itself (the surveyed paradigm) replaces sharing raw model parameters, as in FedAvg, with exchanging soft-label predictions (logits) computed on a shared public/auxiliary dataset, which decouples clients from a shared model architecture and shrinks the communicated payload to the model's output dimension rather than its parameter count.
threat_model: Reported from the survey's own privacy section (Sec. IV.E) and Table VI, not invented. The survey frames the threat as follows: in vanilla FL, exchanging model parameters/gradients lets a malicious server or participant reconstruct sensitive training data via gradient/deep leakage, white-box inference, and membership inference attacks, and lets malicious participants inject undesired information (poisoning). The survey positions FD as a partial mitigation because sharing logits instead of parameters removes the gradient/parameter attack surface (it cites Cronus as claiming white-box inference risk can be eliminated by sharing logits). It also catalogs FD-side defenses: differential privacy (FedKT, FedMD-NFDP noise-free DP, FedHKD Gaussian DP), robust median aggregation of logits against malicious clients (Fedrad), self-purification/attention-guided self-distillation against poisoning (SPFL, FedDefender), consensus/knowledge-vote filtering (KD3A), and input obfuscation (Mix2FLD two-way Mixup, FedNKD noise synthesis). Crucially, the survey does NOT claim FD is secure: it repeatedly notes residual leakage risk, since attacks that exploit information leakage from the logits output itself do not depend on model structure/gradients/parameters.
limitations: As a survey: (a) it is a narrative taxonomy with no head-to-head empirical comparison, so it cannot rank the surveyed methods or quantify the trade-offs it describes; (b) coverage is a snapshot of ~46 works (arXiv v1, Apr 2024) and is FL-classification-centric; the authors themselves note FD for cross-domain/multi-task heterogeneity, class-absent clients, and non-classification tasks is underexplored; (c) the privacy treatment is qualitative — it catalogs attacks and defenses without a formal security analysis or threat-model taxonomy; (d) some surveyed claims (e.g., logit-sharing 'eliminating' white-box inference) are relayed from the cited works rather than independently assessed.

datasets_used: No datasets were used by the paper itself (it runs no experiments); the following recur ACROSS the surveyed methods as reported in Tables I-VI
Vision/general benchmarks: MNIST, FMNIST, EMNIST, FEMNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, Tiny-ImageNet, ImageNet (and long-tailed variants CIFAR10/100-LT, ImageNet-LT)
Medical/healthcare: ADNI, OASIS-1, AIBL (Alzheimer's), ISIC2019, HAM10000 (skin), KiTS19, AMOS22, MSD (segmentation), knee MRI, lung X-ray/CT (COVID-19)
Re-identification/other: MSMT17, DukeMTMC-reID, PACS, Office-Home, UCR2018 time-series
quantitative_results: Survey conducts no experiments; Tables I-VI are categorical, not quantitative, and contain no accuracy figures in the extracted text

Structural counts (approximate, from the catalog tables): Table I catalogs ~50 FD methods for data heterogeneity (labeled Non-IID vs Imbalance, and whether a public dataset is needed); Table II compares ~25 FD methods for model heterogeneity by KD role and aggregation strategy; Table III lists ~14 methods for client-drift; Table IV lists ~19 methods for catastrophic forgetting (local vs global forgetting); Table V catalogs ~40 methods for communication cost (RCI reduce-information / RCR reduce-rounds / both); Table VI lists ~19 privacy-focused FD methods
Survey scope: ~46 cited works per the record metadata; positioned as the first systematic review of KD-in-FL
baselines_compared: None compared experimentally (survey). The following are the conceptual FL reference frameworks that surveyed FD methods are positioned against, not benchmarks run by this paper:
FedAvg (the canonical parameter-averaging FL baseline the survey uses to introduce the FD formulation)
FedProx, FedBN, MOON (other classic privacy-preserving FL algorithms named as reference points)
